title: Factor Levels: Ordering and Releveling
description: Change the order of levels, set reference levels, and create ordered factors.

slides.title: Specifying Level Order
slides.content:
You can define the order of levels explicitly with the `levels` argument. This is important for tables, plots, and modeling (to set a reference category).

```r
size <- c("small", "large", "medium", "small")
f <- factor(size, levels = c("small", "medium", "large"))
```

Now the order is small, medium, large.

slides.title: Custom Level Order
slides.codeSnippet:
size <- c("small", "large", "medium", "small")
f <- factor(size, levels = c("small", "medium", "large"))
print(levels(f))
table(f)

quizData.question: Why would you specify custom level order?

options.text: To control the order in plots and tables

quizData.explanation: Level order affects how tables and plots display the categories, and which level is reference in models.

slides.title: Releveling
slides.action:
The `relevel()` function changes which level is first (the reference level) without reordering all levels.

```r
f <- factor(c("A","B","C"))
f2 <- relevel(f, ref = "B")
```

slides.title: relevel Example
slides.codeSnippet:
f <- factor(c("low","medium","high"))
f2 <- relevel(f, ref = "medium")
print(levels(f))
print(levels(f2))

slides.title: Ordered Factors
slides.content:
For ordinal data (categories with a natural order), use `ordered = TRUE`.

```r
rating <- factor(c("good", "excellent", "poor"), 
                 levels = c("poor", "good", "excellent"), 
                 ordered = TRUE)
```

Now you can compare levels: `rating[1] > rating[3]` makes sense.

slides.title: Ordered Factor
slides.codeSnippet:
temp <- c("hot", "cold", "warm")
temp_factor <- factor(temp, levels = c("cold", "warm", "hot"), ordered = TRUE)
print(temp_factor)
print(temp_factor[1] > temp_factor[2])
